topic: Zero-shot fault diagnosis based on diffusion model and language model
journal: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement (IEEE TIM)

structure.notes: Both examples follow the same 5-section structure (I-V). Section II covers problem definition, background models, and domain shift. Section III has subsections A-D covering overall framework, data preprocessing, model training, and feature generation/diagnosis. Section IV has 2-3 case studies each with dataset description, model training, results comparison, ablation study.

references.intro_ratio: ≥50% of total references must appear in Introduction

required_types: Comparison between traditional and zero-shot fault diagnosis (concept figure)
Overall framework / flowchart of proposed method (full-page width)
t-SNE visualization of generated vs real features (ablation variants)
Accuracy of proposed method under different classifiers
Accuracy comparison with SOTA methods (with std dev)

writing_style.math: Heavy use of equations (10-15 equations), properly numbered
writing_style.abbreviations: Define on first use, e.g., ZSL, ZSFD, DDPM, GAN, etc.
intro_pattern: 1. General introduction to fault diagnosis importance
2. Challenge of scarce fault data → motivate zero-shot learning
3. Review of non-generative ZSL methods for fault diagnosis
4. Review of generative methods (GAN-based) for ZSL/fault diagnosis
5. Limitations of existing methods (domain shift, insufficient semantic learning, etc.)
6. Proposed method overview and how it addresses limitations
experiment_pattern: 5) Fault diagnosis accuracy comparison with SOTA

both_examples_use: Tennessee Eastman Process (TEP) - 15 fault classes, 5 groups
Real hydraulic system - 144 fault types, 3 scenarios (15/25/30 unseen)

key_concepts_shared: Binary fault attributes defined by expert knowledge

formatting.figure_width: single column or double column (framework figures use full width)
